macro.risk_summary: Tourism is holding up, but oil/fuel just jumped hard and Hormuz risk is still live.

metrics.label: Brent Crude
metrics.note: WTI is at $104.23/bbl and up +11.5% in a month as Hormuz disruption reprices supply risk.

metrics.label: USD/MXN
metrics.note: Peso strengthened this week; 30-day direction is still lower for USD/MXN, which helps imported costs.

metrics.label: Mexico Inflation
metrics.note: Latest INEGI-linked reading accelerated from 4.02% in February; restaurants/lodging and food are still hot.

metrics.label: Gasoline (MX)
metrics.note: Diesel is $28.54/L, up +4.6% in a month. Fuel is now a real margin risk for rentals, hauling, and site logistics.

metrics.label: Tourism Demand
metrics.delta: Semana Santa pacing above last year
metrics.note: Quintana Roo is running ~80.7% occupancy with >1.2M visitors projected and ~623 daily flights, though Cancun airport March domestic traffic fell 5.9% YoY.

metrics.label: Geopolitical Risk
metrics.note: Iran/Hormuz shipping risk, sanctions exposure, and emergency stock releases are keeping oil and freight markets jumpy.

geopolitical.title: Hormuz disruption is lifting oil and shipping risk
geopolitical.summary: Analysts now see the Iran war shock flipping the oil market into deficit in 2026, with insurance, sanctions, and tanker access risks still distorting flows.

geopolitical.title: Iran leverage over Hormuz remains a live pricing threat
geopolitical.summary: Even without a full closure, any toll/permission regime around Hormuz effectively hardwires higher crude, freight, and inflation risk.

geopolitical.title: Quintana Roo Semana Santa demand is running above last year
geopolitical.summary: State data shows 80.7% hotel occupancy and more than 1.2 million visitors projected for Semana Santa, with tourism demand still solid despite cost pressure.

macro.bottom_line: Don’t get cute with fuel this week. Tighten Lagoon pricing, watch longer rentals and delivery routes, and build in a fuel buffer on every vehicle move. For Oaxaca, assume transport and imported material costs stay jumpy for now—lock quotes fast, prioritize locally sourced inputs where possible, and avoid drifting bids.
